title.emphasis: The Patronage Incentive and the "Directly Related" Test

section.para: The core principle of Subchapter T is to grant single-tax treatment to income generated from business done with or for patrons.1 This income, when distributed as a patronage dividend, is deductible by the cooperative and taxed only at the patron level.3 In contrast, income from non-patronage sources—such as business with non-members or returns from passive investments—is taxed at the corporate level and then again when distributed to owners, mirroring the double taxation of a standard C corporation.5 This creates a clear and powerful preference for activities that generate patronage-sourced income.

This brings us to your crucial question about reciprocal investments between cooperatives. The classification of income as patronage-sourced hinges on a key legal standard established in case law: the "directly related" test.6 This test holds that for income to be considered patronage-sourced, the transaction producing it must

actually facilitate the cooperative's core marketing, purchasing, or service activities.4 If a transaction merely enhances the overall profitability of the cooperative in an incidental way, the income is generally considered non-patronage.4
Applying this test to inter-cooperative investments reveals the following:

listitem.para: Passive Investment: If Cooperative A simply buys publicly traded shares in Cooperative B as a passive investment to generate a return, the dividend income would almost certainly be classified as non-patronage. The investment is incidental to, not facilitative of, Cooperative A's primary business with its own patrons.4

Strategic Investment: However, if Cooperative A makes a strategic investment in Cooperative B (its primary supplier) specifically to secure a reliable source of inputs for its members, the dynamic changes. In this scenario, the investment is not merely for profit; it "actually facilitates" the accomplishment of Cooperative A's purchasing activities on behalf of its patrons. Following the logic of rulings like Rev. Rul. 74-160, where interest income from a loan to a key supplier was deemed patronage-sourced, a strong argument can be made that the dividend income from this strategic investment is also patronage-sourced.6

section.para: This distinction is the key. It confirms your intuition that reciprocal relationships between cooperatives can, in fact, create patronage-sourced income, provided the investment is structurally integral to the cooperative's mission and not just a passive financial holding.
